visualSignals: Charged particle moving in magnetic field
verbalTriggers: Force on moving charge in magnetic field
nonExamples: Electric force F = qE (no magnetic field)

decisionTree.question: Is the velocity perpendicular to B?
decisionTree.ifYes: Circular motion with F = qvB providing centripetal force

decisionTree.question: Is there also an electric field?
decisionTree.ifNo: Pure magnetic force F = qv × B

steps.instruction: Identify q, v, B and angle between v and B
steps.pitfall: Sign of charge matters for direction
steps.check: Units: q in C, v in m/s, B in T (Tesla)

steps.pitfall: If v ⊥ B, sin(90°) = 1, so F = |q|vB

steps.instruction: Find direction using right-hand rule: thumb = v, fingers = B, palm = F (positive q)
steps.pitfall: For negative q, force is opposite to right-hand rule result
steps.check: F is perpendicular to both v and B

steps.instruction: For circular motion: equate magnetic force to centripetal force qvB = mv²/r
steps.pitfall: Don't forget radius depends on mass and velocity

commonErrors.symptom: Force direction wrong for negative charge
commonErrors.diagnosis: Applied right-hand rule but didn't reverse for negative charge
commonErrors.fix: Right-hand rule gives direction for positive q; for negative q, force is opposite
commonErrors.example: Electron moving right in B field pointing up: RH gives force out of palm, but electron feels force opposite

commonErrors.diagnosis: Didn't equate magnetic force to centripetal force correctly
commonErrors.fix: qvB = mv²/r → r = mv/(qB). Larger mass or velocity → larger radius; larger B or q → smaller radius
commonErrors.example: Proton and electron with same v in same B: r_proton > r_electron because m_p >> m_e

commonErrors.symptom: Magnetic field does work on particle
commonErrors.diagnosis: Fundamental misunderstanding - magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity
commonErrors.fix: F ⊥ v means F · v = 0, so magnetic force never does work (doesn't change kinetic energy)
commonErrors.example: Magnetic field only changes direction of motion, not speed

workedExamples.problem: A proton (q = +e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C) moves at 5.0×10⁶ m/s perpendicular to a 0.20 T magnetic field. Find the force magnitude and direction.

solution.math: Direction: perpendicular to both v and B (use right-hand rule)
solution.explanation: Force direction depends on orientation of v and B

workedExamples.keyInsight: Magnetic force magnitude is simply qvB when v ⊥ B

workedExamples.problem: An electron moving at 2.0×10⁷ m/s enters a 0.10 T magnetic field perpendicular to its velocity. Find the radius of its circular path.

solution.math: Magnetic force provides centripetal force: evB = mv²/r
solution.explanation: For circular motion, net force = centripetal force

workedExamples.keyInsight: Radius proportional to momentum (mv), inversely proportional to field strength and charge

questions.integral: The magnetic force on a moving charge is always:
questions.explanation: F = qv × B, so force is perpendicular to both v and B

questions.integral: An electron and proton with the same velocity enter the same magnetic field perpendicularly. Which has larger orbit radius?
questions.explanation: r = mv/(qB); proton has ~1836× larger mass, same q and v, so much larger r

problems.problem: A charge of 2 μC moves at 1000 m/s at 30° to a 0.5 T field. Find the force.
problems.hint: Use F = qvBsin(θ) when v is not perpendicular to B

problems.problem: In a mass spectrometer, ions with charge +e and different masses are accelerated through potential V then enter magnetic field B. Find the relationship between mass and radius of curvature.

cheatSheet.oneLiner: F = qv × B, magnitude F = |q|vBsin(θ); direction: right-hand rule (reverse for negative q)
cheatSheet.quickCheck: F ⊥ v, so magnetic force does no work (doesn't change speed)

masteryCriteria.executionSpeed: Calculate force and radius in under 5 minutes
selfCheckQuestions: Can I apply the right-hand rule correctly?
Do I reverse direction for negative charges?
Can I derive the cyclotron radius formula?
